blocks.text: Ms. Carol Lewis, Director of Operations, {{fact:f:E-2007-001.client}}
Re: Agreement for Professional Engineering Services, Bridge Deck Rehabilitation, Peck-Jennings
Dear Ms. Lewis:

This letter, when countersigned and returned to our office, will constitute the agreement between {{fact:f:E-2007-001.client}} and Brackenridge Civil Group Inc for professional engineering services in connection with the rehabilitation of the bridge deck carrying the private roadway on your property. We are pleased to have been asked. The terms are set out at some length below so that there is no question later about what is included and what is not.
Scope of Services

Our services are limited to the engineering design and to the documents that carry it. We will perform a condition survey of the existing deck and its approaches, prepare rehabilitation plans and specifications suitable for competitive bidding, and assist you during the bidding period. Construction inspection, materials testing, and any work requiring a structural analysis of the substructure are excluded, and we will quote them separately if you want them. The services included are the following:

items: Condition survey of the deck, wearing surface, joints, railing, and the approach pavement on both sides, documented with field notes and photographs.
Review of the record drawings and any prior inspection reports {{fact:f:E-2007-001.client}} is able to furnish, together with our own field measurements where the record is silent.
Rehabilitation plans and specifications covering deck repair limits, wearing surface, joint replacement, railing, drainage of the deck and the approaches, and traffic control during construction.
A construction cost estimate at the preliminary submittal and again with the final plans.
Assistance during the bidding period, including written responses to bidder questions and the preparation of addenda.

blocks.text: Approach

We will proceed in two submittals. The first is a preliminary set at approximately sixty percent completion, issued for your review and for the review of any agency having jurisdiction over the crossing. The second is the final set, sealed and issued for bidding, carrying our responses to the comments returned on the preliminary. Comments are answered in writing, in the order they were issued, one response to each. That has been our practice for some years and it keeps the record straight when a question comes back a year later.

Field work will be scheduled around your operations. We will need access to the deck and to the underside where it can be reached safely. We will contact your maintenance staff before each visit rather than arrive unannounced.
Project Team

Pamela Warren, Project Engineer, is responsible for the design and is your day-to-day contact at this office. Jason Jordan, Design Technician, will produce the plan set and is responsible for the drawings meeting our standards. Kimberly Branch, Design Technician, will prepare the standard details and the approach grading sheets. I will review the work and seal the plans. Every drawing that leaves this office over our cover sheet has been read by me first, and that will not change on this project.
Commencement and Schedule

Services will commence on {{fact:f:E-2007-001.start}}, subject to our receipt of this letter countersigned. We have laid the work out over approximately four months from commencement, which allows time for your review of the preliminary submittal and for the agency review that follows it. The schedule assumes that the record drawings are furnished at the outset and that weather permits field work in the first weeks. Should either assumption fail, we will tell you promptly and in writing rather than absorb the delay quietly and explain it at the end.
Fees and Payment

Our fee for the scope described above is a fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2007-001.fee}}, which includes our ordinary reimbursable expenses: mileage within the county, plotting, and the reproduction of review sets. Reproduction of bid sets for distribution to contractors is not included and will be invoiced at our cost.

We invoice monthly for the portion of the work completed in the preceding month. Invoices are payable within thirty days of the date rendered, and balances remaining unpaid beyond that period may, at our option, bear interest at the maximum rate permitted by law. Additional services requested by {{fact:f:E-2007-001.client}} and lying outside the scope above will be performed only after the scope and the fee for them have been agreed in writing. We do not begin extra work on a verbal instruction and then present the bill.
Term and Termination

This agreement takes effect on commencement and continues until we have delivered the final sealed plans and completed our assistance during the bidding period, unless terminated earlier. Either party may terminate upon seven days written notice, with or without cause. Upon termination you will pay us for services performed and expenses incurred through the effective date, and we will deliver the work in whatever state it then stands, with the understanding that plans which have not been sealed are not to be used for construction.
Confidentiality

We will hold in confidence the information {{fact:f:E-2007-001.client}} furnishes to us and will not disclose it to a third party except as required to perform the work, or as required by law or by an agency having jurisdiction. This does not extend to information already public or already in our possession. We ask in turn that our drawings, specifications, and calculations be used only for this project. They are instruments of service, they remain our property, and we retain our copyright in them. You may of course keep and use copies for the operation and maintenance of the completed work.
Limitation of Liability

Brackenridge Civil Group will perform its services with the care and skill ordinarily exercised by civil engineers practicing in this locality at this time. We make no other warranty, express or implied. To the fullest extent permitted by law, the total liability of this firm and its employees to {{fact:f:E-2007-001.client}} arising out of this agreement, whether in contract or in tort, shall not exceed the total fee paid to us under it. Neither party shall be liable to the other for consequential damages, including loss of use or loss of profit.
Governing Law

This agreement is governed by the laws of the state in which the project is located, without regard to its conflicts provisions. It is the entire agreement between us on this subject and supersedes any prior understanding, written or verbal. It may be amended only by a writing signed by both parties.

If the foregoing is acceptable, please sign both copies in the space provided below, retain one for your files, and return the other to this office. We will begin as soon as we have it in hand. Should any term require discussion, telephone me and we will take it up before you sign rather than after.
We appreciate the confidence Peck-Jennings has placed in this firm, and we will do the work properly.
Very truly yours,
TO: Pamela Warren, Kimberly Branch
FROM: Jason Jordan
SUBJECT: Bridge Deck Rehabilitation, Peck-Jennings. Project setup and workstreams.

The project file is open and lettered. The start date of record is {{fact:f:E-2007-001.start}}, and everything below runs from that date. This memo covers what we are producing, who has which part, and how the drawings are set up, so that nobody has to ask twice. It is a short schedule and it stays short only if the front end is done properly, which is what this is about.
Objective

{{fact:f:E-2007-001.client}} has retained us for the design and for bid-period assistance, nothing beyond that. What we owe is a sealed plan set and a specification for the rehabilitation of the deck and its approaches, complete enough that a contractor can bid it without a pile of questions and build it without a pile of change orders. There are two submittals, preliminary at sixty percent and final for bidding. Plan the work backward from the preliminary, not forward from today.
Workstreams

items: Field survey and condition documentation. Pamela Warren. Deck, joints, railing, approach pavement, and drainage, measured and photographed. Field books live in the project folder, not in a truck.

Base drawings, layering, and the plotting setup. Mine. The template is the firm's, unchanged. If a layer needs to exist that does not, ask me and I will add it and tell everybody. Do not invent one, and do not move one that is already there.
Standard details and approach grading sheets. Kimberly Branch. Details come out of the detail notebook first. We draw a new one only when there is nothing that fits.
Specifications. Pamela Warren, with the Principal Engineer's review before anything is typed clean.
Cost estimate. Pamela Warren, at the preliminary submittal and again at final.
Correspondence and the project file. Everyone. Anything sent or received gets filed the day it moves, and a fax gets its transmittal sheet stapled to it.

blocks.text: Drawing conventions

Two things worth stating now rather than after they have gone wrong. First, the sheet numbering is fixed at the index and does not get renumbered partway through, because the specification and the estimate will both refer to sheets by number and I am not chasing those references twice. If a sheet has to be added late it gets a letter suffix and lives where it belongs. Second, nothing is plotted for issue from a personal directory. The set plots from the project folder, on the firm's pens, or it does not go out.
The cover sheet is drawn last and it is drawn once. It carries the seal, and it is not a place to experiment with the title block.
Client contact

Carol Lewis, Director of Operations at Peck-Jennings, is the client contact. Anything in writing that goes to her leaves this office over the Principal Engineer's signature, not ours. Telephone questions about site access go to her directly, and they get written in the project file the same day, whatever the answer was. If she asks for something that looks like a change in scope, do not agree to it on the telephone. Write down what she asked for and bring it back here.
Next steps

items: I will open the drawing set and issue the sheet index this week. Nobody starts a sheet before the index exists.
Pamela Warren will telephone to arrange the first site visit and to ask for the record drawings. If the record drawings do not exist, we find that out now rather than at sixty percent.
Kimberly Branch will pull the details we expect to use and mark the ones that need editing.
We will meet in the conference room at the end of next week. Bring what you have, not what you intend to have.

blocks.text: Nothing about this job is unusual. The one thing that will hurt us is a survey that comes back thin and gets discovered late, so it is worth an extra half day in the field now.
Project: Bridge Deck Rehabilitation, Peck-Jennings
Subject: Progress meeting, held at this office at the request of {{fact:f:E-2007-001.client}}.
Minutes prepared by Kimberly Branch.
Present:

items: Pamela Warren, Project Engineer, Brackenridge Civil Group Inc
Jason Jordan, Design Technician, Brackenridge Civil Group Inc
Kimberly Branch, Design Technician, Brackenridge Civil Group Inc
Carol Lewis, Director of Operations, Peck-Jennings

blocks.text: Discussion

Pamela Warren reviewed the condition survey against the record drawings. Delamination of the deck is confined to the two spans nearest the west abutment, and to the shoulder areas rather than the wheel paths. The joints at both abutments are failing and will be replaced. This is consistent with what was assumed when the work was scoped.

Carol Lewis asked whether the crossing can remain open during construction. Pamela Warren said that half-width construction under one-way alternating traffic is workable given the deck width, and that the plans will show it that way, with a note permitting the contractor to propose a full closure if the schedule benefit is real. Carol Lewis said a full closure of more than one working day would not be acceptable and asked that the plans say so. Pamela Warren agreed to add it to the traffic control notes.

Jason Jordan reported that the plan set is laid out, the sheet index is issued, and the sheets are drawn as far as the survey supports. He noted that two sheets of the record drawings furnished to us are illegible where they were faxed, and asked whether legible originals exist. Carol Lewis will check her files.

The preliminary submittal was discussed. It will be issued at sixty percent for review by {{fact:f:E-2007-001.client}} and by the agency having jurisdiction over the crossing. Comments will be answered in writing, in the order they are issued. Nothing raised in the meeting changed the scope of the work or the fee.

Kimberly Branch asked for the intended treatment at the joint header before the detail is drawn, so that it is not drawn twice. Pamela Warren will mark up a sketch. Kimberly Branch also asked which spelling of the client's name is to appear in the title block. Carol Lewis provided the correct form and it will be used on every sheet.

Drainage of the deck was discussed. Pamela Warren said the existing scuppers are undersized and partly blocked, and that the rehabilitation will replace them rather than clean them, since the deck is open in those areas regardless. Carol Lewis asked whether that adds cost. Pamela Warren said it is within the scope as written and does not.

Carol Lewis asked when she would see something. Jason Jordan said the preliminary set is the first thing worth looking at and that an incomplete set circulated early causes more questions than it answers. Carol Lewis accepted this and asked only that she be told before it is transmitted, so that she can arrange her own review. Pamela Warren agreed to telephone in advance.
Action items

rows: Add the traffic control note limiting a full closure to one working day.
Mark up the joint header treatment for drafting.
Locate legible originals of the two illegible record drawing sheets.
Plot a check set of the preliminary sheets for internal review.
Draw the joint header and approach grading details on receipt of the sketch.

blocks.text: The next progress meeting will be arranged by telephone. Corrections to these minutes should be given to Kimberly Branch within one week. Otherwise they stand as written and go in the project file.
Project: Bridge Deck Rehabilitation, Peck-Jennings
Prepared for {{fact:f:E-2007-001.client}} by Jason Jordan, Design Technician, Brackenridge Civil Group Inc.
Reporting period: since our last report.
Status summary

The work is on schedule and within the fee. The design stands at about the midpoint. The condition survey is complete, the plan set is laid out and drawn as far as the survey supports, and the preliminary submittal is in preparation for issue at sixty percent. Nothing has occurred that changes the scope of the work, and we are not asking for anything from you at this time beyond the item noted under Risks.

The condition of the deck is close enough to what was assumed when the work was scoped that the design has not had to be reconsidered. That is the most useful thing in this report. On a rehabilitation the survey is where the schedule is usually lost, and it was not lost here.
Accomplishments this period

items: Pamela Warren completed the condition survey of the deck, joints, railing, approach pavement, and drainage, and reconciled the findings against the record drawings. Delamination is confined to the two spans nearest the west abutment and to the shoulder areas rather than the wheel paths.
The sheet index was issued and the plan set laid out. A check set has been plotted and marked internally.
Kimberly Branch drew the standard details that could be taken from our existing library, and identified the two that require new work.

The traffic control approach was settled at a progress meeting: half-width construction under one-way alternating traffic, with a note permitting the contractor to propose a full closure of not more than one working day.
The specification outline was drafted and is with the Principal Engineer for review.

The record drawings furnished were reviewed sheet by sheet against the field measurements. Where the two disagree, the field measurement governs and the disagreement is noted on the sheet so that a reviewer can see it without having to ask.

blocks.text: A progress meeting was held at this office with your Director of Operations in attendance. Minutes were issued and are in the project file. The items raised there are carried in the design and none of them required a change to the scope.
Upcoming work

items: Complete the sixty percent submittal and transmit it for your review and for the review of the agency having jurisdiction.
Prepare the preliminary construction cost estimate to accompany the submittal.
Draw the joint header and approach grading details.
Receive and answer review comments in writing, in the order they are issued, one response to each.
Advance the specification from outline to draft against the sixty percent sheets, so that the two are consistent before either is issued.
Begin the traffic control sheets, which follow from the half-width approach settled at the progress meeting.

blocks.text: We will telephone before the submittal is transmitted so that your review can be arranged in advance rather than around it. Pamela Warren will also be available to walk the set with your staff if that would be useful, and in our experience an hour spent that way returns more than a written comment does.
Budget

The engagement is a fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2007-001.fee}}. The effort expended to date is in line with the work completed, and we do not foresee exceeding the fee for the scope as written. No additional services have been requested or performed. Should any be requested, we will price the work and obtain your written authorization before beginning it.
Risks

Record drawings. Two of the sheets furnished to us are illegible where they were faxed. If legible originals cannot be located, we will measure the joint and the header in the field. That costs us a day and it is not a change in scope, but it has to happen before the final details are drawn, so it is worth a look in your files.

Agency review. The length of the review of the preliminary submittal is not within our control. The schedule assumes a review of ordinary duration. A long review is the single item most likely to move the completion of the final plans, and it is the one we can do the least about.

Extent of repair. The survey establishes where the deck is sound and where it is not, but the true repair limits are not known until the contractor removes the wearing surface. The plans will handle this with unit price bid items for repair area rather than a lump sum, so that a surprise is priced in advance rather than negotiated after the fact.
Weather. Nothing to report.
Questions on any item in this report may be directed to Pamela Warren at this office.
